narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 40.6% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. Your profile shows you ranked in the third decile for grades in your first year of school—lower than the typical student's fifth or sixth decile position—while your third-year decile was even lower at second, compared to the average around the middle of the pack. Your LSAT score of 36.5 was just a touch below the usual 36.7, your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 stood out positively above the average 3.21, and you come from an upper-middle family income bracket, which is solid. As a white female planning to attend full-time, you're in line with many applicants, though women make up about 44% and full-time students 93% of the group. When the model weighed all this, your first-year decile ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure, more than any other factor. Right behind it, your LSAT score nudged the prediction further in that direction, followed closely by your third-year decile. Your family income and undergraduate GPA came next in influence, each contributing toward the failure prediction, though less forcefully, with your racial background, gender, and full-time status playing smaller roles in the same way. Overall, these early academic deciles set you apart from typical students who pass the bar, who tend to rank higher throughout school. To boost your future odds, focus on strengthening the areas that mattered most, like retaking the LSAT for a higher score if possible, since it's a key influencer you might improve with targeted prep. Building stronger study habits now could help mimic the higher decile performance of successful students when it comes to bar prep. Your above-average undergrad GPA is a great foundation—lean into that strength with dedicated bar exam practice to shift the prediction in your favor next time. Keep pursuing full-time status if it fits your plan, as it aligns with most passers. With these steps, you can realistically improve your profile for another shot at admission.
